questions.stem: A public health nurse is conducting a windshield survey of a new community. Which activities are part of this community needs assessment method?

options.text: Administering surveys to a random sample of 500 residents about health behaviors
Driving or walking through the community to observe housing, businesses, transportation, green space, safety indicators, and visible health resources
Reviewing hospital admission records to identify the top 5 diagnoses in the zip code
Interviewing community leaders at the health department and city council

questions.rationale: A windshield survey is a direct observational assessment of a community conducted by driving or walking through it. Observations include: housing conditions (quality, density, vacancy), businesses and industries, transportation availability, green space and recreational facilities, visible health hazards (pollution, liquor stores, food deserts), people present (age groups, homelessness), religious institutions, schools, healthcare facilities, and safety indicators. This method provides rapid, first-hand community data before formal surveys or record reviews. It complements quantitative data (vital statistics, epidemiologic data) with qualitative environmental observations.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Administering surveys to a random sample of 500 residents about health behaviors, rather than acting on This method provides rapid, first-hand community data before formal surveys or record reviews. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Driving or walking through the community to observe housing, businesses, transportation, green space, safety indicators, because delaying bedside safety action can worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Reviewing hospital admission records to identify the top 5 diagnoses in the zip code, even though the higher-risk finding is safety indicators. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Driving or walking through the community to observe housing, businesses, transportation, green space, safety indicators, is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: Interviewing community leaders at the health department and city council is not the best answer, even though the higher-risk finding is A windshield survey is a direct observational assessment of a community conducted by driving or walking through it. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bedside safety action while clinical safety risk progresses.

questions.takeaway: Windshield survey = observational community assessment method; nurse drives/walks through community to document housing, services, hazards, and health resources as a starting point for needs assessment.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The nurse should prioritize A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10 is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Age >65 and female gender, rather than acting on Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic; otherwise medication toxicity remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29, while the decisive cue is Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic because delaying medication-safety intervention can worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The priority is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic, not a step that delays stabilization of medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea would be unsafe, rather than acting on Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube, but the stem cue is Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The correct path is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider is not the best answer, without accounting for BP is 94/58, HR 118. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so It is less defensible because Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion is not the best answer, even though the higher-risk finding is BP is 94/58, HR 118. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The correct path is Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The nurse should prioritize Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement; otherwise bleeding or anticoagulation injury remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: This choice focuses on Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses, while the decisive cue is A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound because delaying circulation and perfusion support can worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.b: This option makes Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, and misses that Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate. Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.' is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, while the decisive cue is therapeutic communication safety. Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with 'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.' is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on therapeutic communication safety. The priority is 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.', not a step that delays stabilization of clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: 'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.' would not address the priority, and misses that therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.' better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, instead of responding to Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions. The nurse should prioritize Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer, and misses that Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80 would be unsafe, while the decisive cue is Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: Selecting HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48 would be unsafe, rather than acting on Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72 is not the best answer, even though the higher-risk finding is RR 40 is elevated. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: The reasoning trap in Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. The correct path is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. It is less defensible because Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.
